scenarioName: Diabetic Ketoacidosis — Kussmaul Respirations and Profound Dehydration

scenarioStory.history: 22-year-old female nursing student with a 12-year history of type-1 diabetes (insulin pump) developed a sore throat and low-grade fever 3 days ago and stopped eating. She continued some long-acting insulin (Tresiba) but reduced her pump bolus rates because she was 'not eating much,' and forgot to change the pump's infusion set 5 days ago — past the recommended 3-day change. Over the past 24 hours she developed progressive polyuria, polydipsia, nausea, several episodes of vomiting, diffuse mid-abdominal pain, and rapid deep breathing. Roommate found her this morning very weak, confused, with 'really fast deep breathing and her breath smelled weird.' Per home glucometer, blood glucose has been reading 'HIGH' (>500 mg/dL) for hours. No alcohol or drug use; no recent travel.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: EMS finds a 22-year-old type-1 diabetic in florid DKA: profound dehydration, Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath, altered mental status, mild compensated shock, and likely failed insulin pump infusion site as the trigger. The clinical picture is classic. This is the initial contact phase: dispatch, SAMPLE history, scene findings, and a focused metabolic assessment drive the response. → Fluids reassessed: After 20 mL/kg of NS, the patient's perfusion is improving — heart rate trending down, EtCO2 trending up, and mental status is somewhat clearer. Glucose remains markedly elevated; correction is the hospital's job. Continued fluids at maintenance rate, transport ongoing.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Progressive nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, weakness, and altered mental status in a known type-1 diabetic

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: EMS finds a 22-year-old type-1 diabetic in florid DKA: profound dehydration, Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath, altered mental status, mild compensated shock, and likely failed insulin pump infusion site as the trigger. The clinical picture is classic. This is the initial contact phase: dispatch, SAMPLE history, scene findings, and a focused metabolic assessment drive the response.
relatedChecklist: Recognize DKA: hyperglycemia + Kussmaul + fruity breath + dehydration
Confirm blood glucose (>250 mg/dL = treat per Hyperglycemia protocol)
Maintain airway; head of bed elevated if mental status declines further
O2 to maintain SpO2 94-98% (currently 98%; nasal cannula adjunct)
Establish two large-bore IVs for fluid resuscitation
NS 20 mL/kg = 1160 mL bolus at 1000 mL/hr; reassess
12-lead EKG (look for hyperkalemia: peaked T waves)
Place waveform EtCO2 (low values = severe acidosis)
Do NOT give insulin in the field; do NOT remove pump (still delivering basal)
Transport with notification (DKA, glucose, vitals, EtCO2)

scenarioEvents.name: Fluids reassessed
scenarioEvents.description: After 20 mL/kg of NS, the patient's perfusion is improving — heart rate trending down, EtCO2 trending up, and mental status is somewhat clearer. Glucose remains markedly elevated; correction is the hospital's job. Continued fluids at maintenance rate, transport ongoing.
relatedChecklist: Reassess vitals + mental status q5min after each bolus
Watch for mental status worsening (cerebral edema warning)
Continue cardiac monitor for K+ changes (peaked T or wide QRS)

scenarioEvents.name: Cerebral edema concern
scenarioEvents.description: After two rapid 1L pressure-bolus boluses without reassessment, the patient becomes more lethargic, harder to arouse, with a unilateral sluggish pupil and complaints of headache before becoming nonverbal. This is the rare-but-feared cerebral edema complication of overly rapid DKA correction. Crew must stop fluids, elevate the head of bed, manage airway, and notify receiving facility immediately.
relatedChecklist: Stop the IV fluid bolus immediately; convert to TKO
Elevate head of bed to reduce ICP
Reassess airway; BVM if respirations <10 or SpO2 falling
Emergent notification: 'DKA with concern for cerebral edema'
